description: This column (public.agent_models.agent_type) classifies each AI coding agent model into a specific agent category/type (e.g., the kind of coding agent or workflow role it supports) for use by Overlord’s management, coordination, and review layer.
This column stores the identifier of an AI coding agent model (the specific model a given agent type uses) in the Overlord agent models table.
This column contains the human-friendly name (display label) for each AI coding agent model (identified by model_id) used within the Overlord agent management and coordination system.
This column is about the identifier for the AI coding agent whose session is being recorded in the public.agent_sessions table.

This column stores the external web URL associated with an individual AI agent session (i.e., the link to the session’s externally accessible page/endpoint), used by Overlord to reference and coordinate work across agent sessions.

This column stores the external session identifier assigned by an outside system/provider for each AI coding agent session, allowing Overlord to correlate agent activity and work orchestration with the corresponding external session.
This column is about a unique identifier (key) for each application feature in the app_features dataset.

This column contains the human-readable feature names for the publicly available application features (e.g., the names of the Overlord AI agent management, work coordination, and review capabilities).

examples: Explains the purpose of the feature and how it helps the Overlord AI coordinate coding agents by assigning tasks, routing requests, and tracking progress across the workflow.
Describes a work orchestration capability that breaks down large engineering goals into smaller agent tasks, schedules execution, and resolves dependencies between steps.
Details how the review layer aggregates outputs from multiple agents, performs consistency checks, and surfaces actionable feedback for improvements before changes are merged.
Provides an overview of the communication feature that standardizes message formats between agents, maintains context, and records decisions for auditability.
Explains how the feature performs task triage by interpreting user intent, identifying required code areas, and selecting the most suitable agent roles for execution.
Describes a capability that monitors agent execution in real time, detects stalled or failing tasks, and triggers retries or alternative strategies automatically.
Outlines how the feature manages shared workspace state, including file diffs, generated artifacts, and temporary branches used by coding agents.
Explains an approval workflow where a human or automated policy reviews agent proposals, verifies tests, and gates promotion to higher environments.
Details how the feature captures review rationale, including why changes were suggested, what risks were identified, and how the agent addressed edge cases.
Describes a capability for summarizing agent work into concise progress updates, including completed items, blockers, and next recommended actions.
Explains how the feature enforces coding conventions and linting standards by running automated checks and requesting agent revisions when violations occur.
Details a mechanism for dependency and context management, ensuring agents operate with the correct repository state, configuration, and relevant prior decisions.
Describes the feature that performs automated unit/integration test execution, reports results, and requests targeted fixes from the responsible agents.
Explains how the review component detects potential regressions by comparing before/after behavior, reviewing affected modules, and validating with existing test suites.
Details a structured escalation path when tasks cannot be completed, including summarizing failure reasons, proposing next steps, and involving higher-level reviewers.
Describes a feature that supports multi-agent collaboration by allowing agents to hand off partial implementations, annotate assumptions, and request clarifications.
Explains how the system maintains an execution log of agent actions, commands run, and artifacts produced to support debugging and traceability.
Details how the feature implements policy-based constraints (e.g., security, privacy, licensing), preventing agents from generating prohibited changes.
Describes a capability that performs change impact analysis, identifying which files and systems are affected and prioritizing review effort accordingly.
Explains how the feature provides final synthesized recommendations, combining agent outputs and review feedback into a coherent plan for delivery.
description: This column is about a textual explanation of each application feature (the feature’s purpose and what it does) for the Overlord AI coding-agents management, work coordination, and review layer.

This column is about the type/category of each stored artifact generated or used by the Overlord AI coding agent workflow (e.g., the kind of item being tracked such as code, instructions, review output, logs, or other artifact classifications).

examples: Code Diff: Refactor Artifact Label Formatter
Workflow Run Log — Artifact Validation Stage
description: This column is about the human-readable label or title associated with each artifact stored in public.artifacts, identifying what the artifact is (e.g., a document/code output/resource) as part of the Overlord AI-coding-agent management and review workflow.

This column is about the URI (resource location/link) associated with each artifact stored in the public.artifacts table, identifying where the artifact can be accessed or retrieved as part of the Overlord AI coding agents management, coordination, and review workflow.

examples:
{"artifact_type":"text","label":"README","uri":"ipfs://bafybeigdyrreadme","storage_path":"s3://artifacts-prod/2026/05/21/readme.txt","encoding":"utf-8","created_at":"2026-05-21T10:15:30Z","content":"# Project Nova

Nova is a lightweight indexing service.

## Quickstart
1. `make build`
2. `make test`
3. `docker compose up`

## Configuration
- `NOVA_PORT`
- `NOVA_MAX_CONN`

## License
MIT"}

{"artifact_type":"text/markdown","label":"architecture-notes","uri":"https://example.org/docs/architecture.md","storage_path":"/data/artifacts/architecture.md","encoding":"utf-8","content":"## Architecture Notes

This service uses: 
- a queue for ingestion
- a worker pool for parsing
- a cache for embeddings

### Failure modes
- retryable upstream timeouts
- dead-letter queue after 5 attempts

### Observability
- structured logs
- OpenTelemetry traces
- Prometheus metrics"}

{"artifact_type":"application/xml","label":"kube-manifest","uri":"s3://manifests/k8s/api-deployment.yaml","storage_path":"s3://artifacts-prod/manifests/api-deployment.yaml","encoding":"utf-8","content":"apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api-worker
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api-worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: api-worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: api-worker
        image: ghcr.io/example/api-worker:2.0.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: LOG_LEVEL
          value: "info"
"}

{"artifact_type":"application/x-yaml","label":"feature-flags","uri":"https://example.com/config/flags.yaml","storage_path":"gs://bucket/config/flags.yaml","encoding":"utf-8","content":"service:
  billing_v2: true
  experimental_search: false
  rate_limit_strategy: "token_bucket"
region_overrides:
  eu-west-1:
    experimental_search: true

rollout:
  cohort_percent: 25
  start_time: "2026-05-20T09:00:00Z""}

{"artifact_type":"text/plain","label":"error-report","uri":"https://example.com/reports/incident-4812.txt","storage_path":"s3://artifacts-prod/incidents/incident-4812.txt","encoding":"utf-8","content":"INCIDENT 4812
Timestamp: 2026-05-21T03:44:10Z
Service: api-worker
Impact: Elevated 5xx for ~18 minutes

Root cause (initial):
- upstream dependency returned malformed JSON

Actions taken:
- deployed patch to validate response schema
- enabled safer retry/backoff

Notes:
User-facing error: "Something went wrong"
"}

{"artifact_type":"text/yaml","label":"sla-policy","uri":"ipfs://bafybeipolicysla","storage_path":"s3://artifacts-prod/policies/sla_policy.yaml","encoding":"utf-8","content":"sla:
  availability_percent: 99.95
  measurement_window: "30d"
  error_budget:
    burn_rate_fast: 14
    burn_rate_slow: 2
alerts:
  - name: "ConsistentErrors"
    threshold: 1.0
    route: "oncall""}

{"artifact_type":"text/plain","label":"license-notice","uri":"https://example.com/notices/THIRD_PARTY.txt","storage_path":"/data/artifacts/licenses/THIRD_PARTY.txt","encoding":"utf-8","content":"Third-Party Notices

This product includes software developed by:
- Example Contributors
- Open Source Initiative

License texts are included in the distribution.
"}
description: This column is about the full stored contents of each artifact (e.g., the text/code/data payload associated with an artifact), located in the public.artifacts table and linked to fields like artifact_type, label, uri, and storage_path.
This column is about the filesystem or object-storage location (storage path) where each artifact’s content is stored for use by the Overlord AI coding agent management and review workflow.

This column is about the unique identifier (user code) for users in the public.auth_grants table, linking permissions/grants to the specific user associated with the Overlord AI coding-agent management and coordination project.

This column is about the name of the client associated with a given user_code in the auth grants data, which is used to manage and coordinate access for people using AI coding agents under the Overlord system.
This column is about a stable, URL-friendly unique identifier (slug) for each changelog entry in the public.changelog_entries table.

examples: Release 1.24.0 — Improved matchmaking and faster queue processing
Hotfix: Fix crash when loading saved game settings
Update documentation — Add integration steps for Overlord webhooks
Security patch: Rotate API tokens and tighten auth validation
Performance upgrade — Optimize database queries for leaderboard pages
New feature: Add team-based roles and permissions management
UI change — Redesign dashboard filters and sorting controls
Refactor: Migrate changelog indexing to background workers
Bugfix: Resolve incorrect time zone display in activity feed
Change log update — Adjust retention policy for audit events
Release 1.23.3 — Stabilize streaming uploads and resume support
Feature: Introduce advanced search for missions and objectives
Improvement — Reduce memory usage during bulk export
Compatibility update — Support latest client SDK version
Admin tooling — Add CSV import validation and preview step
Fix: Correct permission checks for shared projects
Release 1.22.1 — Add rate limiting for public endpoints
Observability update — Enhance structured logs with request IDs
Rollback: Revert problematic feature flag rollout for notifications
Maintenance — Upgrade third-party dependencies and security fixes
description: This column contains the human-readable title of each entry in the public changelog for the project (Overlord), describing what change or update was made.

examples: Improved agent handoff workflow to reduce duplicated tasks during coordination
Added support for assigning reviewers per work item to streamline AI code review
Updated Overlord work queue logic to prioritize urgent agent requests
Enhanced changelog summaries to better describe agent-management changes
Introduced configurable retry policy for failed agent actions and fetches
Refined review status transitions to prevent stuck items after approval
Added audit metadata to agent task runs for easier troubleshooting
Implemented batching for agent updates to reduce API calls during spikes
Improved role-based access checks for work coordination and review operations
Added timeouts and circuit breaking around long-running agent tool calls
Updated UI-facing summary generation to include affected component names
Introduced conflict detection when multiple agents attempt same work item
Enhanced reviewer assignment rules with fallback reviewers and notifications
Added visibility for agent queue position in coordination endpoints
Improved summarization heuristics to highlight impact on code review outcomes
Implemented pagination improvements for browsing historical changelog entries
Added support for cancelling agent tasks and propagating cancellation to work items
Refined scheduler to balance workloads across multiple agent pools
Improved consistency of changelog entry wording for better searchability
Added metrics tracking for review latency and agent execution durations
description: This column contains a short human-readable summary of each changelog entry related to Overlord, describing the purpose or impact of the change for AI coding agent management, work coordination, or review functionality.

examples:
Update: Added automatic task assignment rules for Overlord AI agents based on project tags and workload.

  • New rule types: Priority, Skill Match, Capacity
  • Improved agent routing latency by ~18%

Developers can now preview the assignment plan before publishing changes.

Fix: Resolved an issue where merged PR summaries were sometimes duplicated in the coordination timeline.

Logs now include a correlation ID for easier troubleshooting.

  • Deduplication on merge_event
  • Updated UI timeline ordering

Feature: Introduced “Reviewer Notes” synced with each agent review task.

Notes appear inline during review and are included in exported reports.

  • Markdown support
  • Comment threading
  • Permission checks for internal vs external reviewers

Maintenance: Upgraded the changelog rendering pipeline to normalize HTML entities and reduce XSS risk.

New sanitization rules were applied to <script> and inline event handlers.

No action required.

Update: Enhanced work coordination by adding a “Blockers” section to the agent status card.

  • Blockers can be linked to Jira/Linear issues
  • Status summaries generated nightly

Team leads can filter by blocker severity.

Security: Rotated signing keys used for agent-to-agent message verification.

All messages are now validated against the latest key set.

  • Key rotation schedule: 30 days
  • Grace period for older signatures: 24 hours

Performance: Optimized retrieval of review artifacts for large repositories.

Reduced initial load time by approximately 35% for projects with >10k files.

  • Caching for file manifests
  • Pagination for diffs

Change: Workflows now support multi-step approvals before an agent can finalize a deliverable.

  • Approval stages: Tech Lead → QA → Product
  • Audit trail recorded for each decision

Existing projects were migrated to 1-stage approvals.

Fix: Corrected timezone drift in changelog timestamps shown in the coordination dashboard.

Timestamps are now rendered using the project’s configured timezone and stored in UTC.

  • Resolved DST offset bug
  • Backfilled display values for recent entries

Feature: Added “Runbook Attachments” to agent tasks.

Agents can reference internal documentation directly from the task card.

  • Supported: PDF, TXT, Markdown
  • Automatic link previews

Update: Improved review rubric configuration with clearer scoring categories.

  • Categories: Correctness, Safety, Maintainability, Test Coverage
  • Weighting stored per repository
  • Export includes rubric breakdown

Fix: Addressed a race condition where agent status updates could overwrite each other during high-frequency events.

Introduced optimistic concurrency checks on status transitions.

  • Prevents lost updates
  • Better conflict messaging

Maintenance: Refreshed the HTML changelog sanitizer to allow safe <code> blocks with language hints.

Example syntax: const x = 1;

All previous entries remain compatible.

Feature: Added “Review SLA” tracking for agent tasks.

  • Configurable SLA window per team
  • Automatic alerts for overdue reviews
  • Summary emailed to channel at end of SLA

Update: Enhanced agent handoffs with structured context snapshots.

Snapshots include: recent commits, open PRs, failing tests, and relevant discussions.

  • Context size capped with truncation rules
  • Deterministic formatting for reproducibility

Fix: Corrected hyperlink rendering in changelog entries where relative URLs were missing base paths.

Links now resolve consistently across staging and production environments.

  • Relative paths normalized
  • Malformed anchors handled gracefully

Change: Introduced a “Read-only Mode” for external stakeholders.

External users can view changelog entries and review summaries but cannot edit task metadata.

  • Role-based access updated
  • UI disables write controls

Performance: Reduced load time of the coordination dashboard by batching changelog queries.

New query uses pagination and avoids N+1 lookups.

  • ~22% fewer database round trips
  • Improved cache hit rate

Security: Added Content Security Policy (CSP) headers for changelog views.

This mitigates inline script risks and tightens allowed sources.

  • CSP: script-src 'self'
  • Trusted types enabled for HTML rendering

Maintenance: Standardized terminology in Overlord AI status messages.

“Agent Planning” is now labeled as “Work Plan” across the UI and changelog exports.

  • Updated templates
  • Backfilled historical labels where applicable
description: This column contains the HTML-formatted (rich-text/markup) body content of each entry in the public.changelog_entries table, describing changes or updates relevant to the Overlord AI coding agents management, work coordination, and review project.
This column is about the changelog entry’s version identifier/number, indicating which release or iteration of the Overlord project the specific changelog entry belongs to.

This column stores the identifier of the external service or integration provider associated with a public connection (e.g., the source platform for an AI coding agent integration) so the system can manage and coordinate reviews across different providers.

This column contains the human-readable name (display name) for a connection associated with an AI-coding-agent provider, used in the Overlord management/work coordination and review layer to identify who or what the connection represents.

This column stores the OAuth device code used in the device authorization flow for the Overlord service to authenticate a user. It is part of the record that links a user’s authorization attempt (and resulting tokens) to a specific device authorization session.

This column stores the user identifier (user_code) associated with a device authentication/authorization record in the Overlord system, linking the device-issued auth data (e.g., tokens and PKCE/state values) to the specific user who initiated or is using the AI coding agent workflow.

This column (public.device_auth_codes.access_token) stores the OAuth access token issued for a device authentication flow. It represents the short-lived credential the AI-coding-agent/user uses to access protected Overlord services on behalf of the authenticated device/user.

This column stores the OAuth refresh token associated with a public.device authentication record. It is used to obtain new access tokens for an AI coding agent user/session without requiring the user to re-authenticate, as part of Overlord’s device-based OAuth flow.

This column stores the PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) code verifier value associated with a device authorization flow for an AI-coding-agent user. It is used later to validate the authorization exchange by matching it with a corresponding PKCE code challenge during OAuth token retrieval.

This column stores the OAuth/OIDC state value (the anti-CSRF “oauth_state”) associated with a device-based login request for Overlord’s AI-coding-agent management. It is generated when initiating the device authorization flow and later validated when completing the token exchange to ensure the response matches the original request.

This column stores the IP address of the client/requester that initiated the device authorization flow for public.device_auth_codes (e.g., the source IP recorded when generating the device auth code).
This column is about the name of the person who submitted an early access request for Overlord, the management, work coordination, and review layer for users of AI coding agents.
This column stores the email addresses of users who submitted an early access request for Overlord, the management, work coordination, and review layer for people using AI coding agents.
This column stores the role of each requester in the Overlord early access program (e.g., what kind of user or stakeholder they are), for people using AI coding agents.
This column is about the origin/source of a given AI agent execution request (i.e., which client/user/system initiated it) for the Overlord work coordination and review layer.

This column identifies which AI coding agent (by a specific agent identifier) is associated with a given execution request, so the Overlord management layer can route coordination, execution, and subsequent review to the correct agent.

This column stores the identifier of the AI model that the execution request should use for the requested coding/agent work (e.g., which LLM/model variant the Overlord coordination layer will route the task to).

This column stores the selected AI agent "thinking level" (i.e., the requested reasoning/effort intensity) for a given execution request in the public.execution_requests table, influencing how the AI coding agent should plan or reason during the Overlord orchestration.

examples: Execution failed: API timeout while calling OpenAI (request_id=req-9f1a2c). Deadline exceeded after 30s.
Could not start agent: missing environment variable OPENAI_API_KEY.
Tool execution error in sandbox: /usr/local/bin/git not found (exit_code=127).
Compilation step failed: TypeScript error TS2307: Cannot find module 'react'.
Database constraint violation: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "execution_requests_pk".
Unhandled exception in agent loop: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'content').
Rate limited by provider: HTTP 429 Too Many Requests. Retry-after: 10s.
Network error: ECONNRESET when fetching repository from https://github.com/org/repo.
Authentication failed: 401 Unauthorized when calling internal tool "vector_search".
Workspace preparation failed: disk quota exceeded while writing /tmp/agent-run/trace.json.
Runtime error: Python process exited with code 1. stderr: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'.
LLM output validation failed: expected JSON but received plain text. Output: "I think it should work"
Patch application failed: hunk failed to apply at line 42 in file src/index.ts (git apply error).
Tests failed: 3 failing tests in pytest (exit_code=1). First failure: AssertionError at tests/test_auth.py:88.
Agent aborted due to safety policy: disallowed content detected in prompt (category: "secrets").
Exceeded maximum tool calls: reached limit of 25 tool invocations for this execution request.
Timeout during long-running task: command "npm test" exceeded 120s limit (exit_code=124).
Unexpected provider response: HTTP 500 from LLM service. Body: {"error":"internal_error"}.
File upload failed: could not read artifact at path /mnt/data/build/output.zip (ENOENT).
description: This column stores the most recent error information (e.g., error message/stack or failure details) related to an execution request in the public.execution_requests table, such as why the AI coding agent run failed or could not be completed.

This column stores an idempotency key used to uniquely identify an execution request so the system can safely deduplicate retries and prevent the same AI coding agent task from being executed multiple times.
This column stores the username associated with a target host’s SSH credentials used by the project (Overlord) to manage and coordinate work performed by AI coding agents.
This column stores the filesystem path (on the executing machine or container) to the private SSH key file used to authenticate for the configured execution target.

This column stores the SSH public key fingerprint for the execution target’s SSH credentials, allowing Overlord to reliably identify and match the correct public key when coordinating secure connections for AI coding agent execution.

This column contains the SSH host key fingerprint for the target execution host that the AI coding agents will connect to over SSH, used to verify the server identity and prevent man-in-the-middle attacks.

This column stores the identifier (tag) of the secure enclave used to protect or retrieve the SSH credentials (e.g., the key material) for the public execution target associated with the user's AI-coding-agent environment.

This column stores a device fingerprint used to identify or distinguish the specific client device associated with a record in the Overlord system (e.g., to support attribution, security checks, or matching execution contexts for AI coding-agent activity).

This column is about mapping a specific placeholder key (e.g., a template variable identifier) to an execution target so the Overlord system can fill in or interpret the correct parameters when coordinating and reviewing work performed by AI coding agents.
This column is about the human-readable name of an execution target used by the Overlord system to identify and coordinate which AI coding agent workloads should run.

This column stores the operating environment or technology platform associated with each public execution target (e.g., the target OS/runtime or platform where the AI coding/agent work is coordinated and executed).
This column is about the type/category of the AI coding agent that the feed post is associated with within the Overlord management, work coordination, and review workflow.

examples: Overlord Update: v1.8.3 rollout and new agent scheduling options
Work Coordination: prioritize backlog items for the next sprint—review needed
Review Request: audit my agent tool permissions before deployment
Overlord Maintenance Window: services may be offline from 02:00–02:30 UTC
Status Summary: AI coding agents have completed 12/15 review tasks
Task Assignment: assign me to implement the codegen retry logic
Feedback Needed: update the prompt templates for better function calling
Overlord Ruleset Change: new policy for secure file access in agent runs
Bug Triage: investigate intermittent failures in the build verification step
Release Notes: Overlord now supports multi-repo workspace indexing
Review Queue: 3 pull requests are ready for your approval
Coordination Check-in: confirm ownership for the new coding agent endpoints
Security Update: rotating secrets for Overlord worker pools—read before running
Performance Watch: monitoring indicates slower token streaming in agent responses
Documentation Reminder: refresh the README with updated agent workflows
Agent Ops: runbooks updated—incident response steps revised for 2026
QA Findings: failing tests linked to schema validation—need a fix
Overlord Integration: connect CI webhooks for automatic review triggers
Progress Report: completed migration of feed pipelines to the new queue
Final Review: approve the release candidate and confirm rollout checklist
description: This column is about the headline or brief summary of each feed post related to Overlord—such as updates, work coordination items, and review-related information for users working with AI coding agents.

examples: ✅ Weekly agent coordination update: The Planner agent drafted the implementation plan for the new review queue. The Coder agent created the endpoints, and the Verifier agent ran the test suite (all green). Next step: tighten the review SLA metrics and backfill historical queue durations.

Prompt received: “Summarize the current AI coding workflow and propose improvements for the Overlord management layer.” Draft summary: route tasks by risk level, add retry logic for flaky tests, and persist review notes per agent run.

Review comment: The PR is close, but the error handling in `submit_review()` is inconsistent. Please align status codes across services and ensure idempotency keys are used when re-submitting review tasks.

New run scheduled: Orchestrator will dispatch tasks to Planner → Coder → Verifier. Estimated time: 6–9 minutes. Watchpoints: environment variables, missing migrations, and ensuring the review artifacts are stored with the correct run_id.

Update: The Coder agent added the missing indexes, but the query planner still shows a sequential scan on `feed_posts`. Verifier suggests adding a composite index on `(user_id, created_at)` and re-running EXPLAIN ANALYZE.

Testing notes: AI agent workflow passed unit tests, but the integration suite failed on one scenario involving concurrent review submissions. Root cause suspected: race condition in queue locking—please add a transaction boundary or advisory lock.

Agent sync: Overlord layer now enforces a three-stage gate—(1) spec compliance, (2) code quality checks, (3) verification artifacts present. Awaiting Coder follow-up to include the missing lint configuration.
User feedback: “The agent summaries are too verbose.” Action: reduce summary length to ~120 tokens, include only decisions, assumptions, and next steps. Verifier to confirm summaries remain accurate.
Design review: Replace the current retry loop with exponential backoff and circuit breaker behavior. Ensure the Orchestrator records retry attempts in the audit log for postmortem analysis.

Prompt: “Generate a checklist for reviewing agent PRs.” Checklist includes: reasonableness of changes, regression risk, test coverage, documentation updates, and validation that review metadata (run_id, agent_id) is persisted correctly.

Daily status: Planner identified 5 tasks; Coder completed 3; Verifier flagged 2. Remaining work: implement pagination for the feed endpoint and ensure body content sanitization meets policy requirements.

Review note: The migration adds a NOT NULL constraint for `public.feed_posts.body` but existing rows may violate it. Please add a default value or backfill existing records before applying the constraint.
Automation update: Overlord management layer now tags agent runs with `workflow_version`. Verifier confirmed reproducibility by re-running the same workflow against a fixed snapshot dataset.

Bug report from Verifier: The agent workflow occasionally duplicates review tasks after a timeout. Suspect: task enqueue is not idempotent. Fix requested: enforce unique constraint on `(run_id, task_type)`.

Stakeholder message: “Keep the review interface consistent.” Proposal: standardize UI fields (priority, owner agent, status, last updated) and ensure the body text is rendered with identical formatting across views.
Code review summary: The refactor improves readability, but the new abstraction changes behavior when existing reviews contain empty notes. Please add tests for empty/whitespace-only body content.

Ops update: There was a temporary queue lag during peak hours. Orchestrator scaled to 6 workers; processing caught up within 4 minutes. Next: monitor queue depth and tune worker autoscaling thresholds.

Prompt: “Assess whether the workflow properly separates planning, coding, and verification responsibilities.” Findings: responsibilities are mostly separated, but the Coder agent currently writes verifier notes. Recommend adding a schema-level separation.

Verification artifact: Attached logs show successful execution of lint, unit tests, and contract tests. Remaining concern: performance regression in the review feed query; needs one more optimization pass.

Review follow-up: Please update the agent response template to include a concise “Assumptions” section and a “Next Actions” list. Verifier should validate that the template renders correctly for multi-paragraph bodies.
description: This column is about the main text content of each feed post (the post body) describing messages, updates, prompts, or reviews related to agent coordination and AI coding agent workflows within the Overlord management/review layer.

examples: Overall the Overlord coordination layer is helpful, but I ran into delays when multiple agents requested reviews at the same time.
Had trouble getting clear status updates from the management layer—tasks were marked complete even though code review hadn’t finished.
The agent handoff between planning and implementation felt smooth, but the final review summary was missing key acceptance criteria.
I noticed the review layer sometimes reopens tasks without explaining why, which caused duplicate work across agents.
The issue triage worked well at first, but after a few iterations the system started grouping unrelated bugs into one ticket.
Suggestions from the reviewer agent were actionable, but some recommendations conflicted with earlier constraints provided by management.
Encountered an error when the agent tried to fetch context for a previous PR; it returned outdated files.
Coordination was solid during the first sprint; however, the workload balancing seemed to favor one agent repeatedly.
The feedback notes were too general—could we include specific file paths and line references when flagging problems?
The system did a good job keeping me informed, though I’d like more transparency about which agent is currently responsible.
Review layer caught a security concern late in the process; earlier automated checks would have prevented wasted iterations.
I liked the structured feedback format, but the 'next steps' section often repeated the same instructions verbatim.
The Overlord project’s agent management handled branching tasks well, but merging conflicts weren’t clearly communicated.
The prompt for agents to confirm assumptions was useful; still, I had to manually correct several incorrect dependencies.
We saw occasional timeouts when running tests through the management layer, which interrupted the review workflow.
The system prioritized enhancements over bug fixes at times; would be good to make that priority more configurable.
Review comments were thorough, but it would help to tag severity levels consistently (e.g., low/medium/high).
The handoff between review and implementation caused a loop—changes were requested, applied, then reverted automatically.
I appreciated the ability to attach additional context for the agents, but the UI/UX for adding it could be clearer.
At times the agent description of the problem didn’t match the actual failing tests; better alignment on logs would help.
description: This column contains free-text feedback descriptions related to the "Overlord" project—specifically notes about experiences, issues, suggestions, or observations concerning the management, work coordination, and review layer for users of AI coding agents.
This column contains the name of the source file associated with a recorded file change event (the specific file that was added/modified/removed as part of the Overlord AI coding agent workflow).

This column is about the filesystem or repository path (location) of each changed file in the project’s “file changes” dataset, typically identifying where the file lives so it can be mapped to its related filename, label, summary, rationale (why), impact, and other review metadata.
This column is about the classification label applied to a specific file change recorded in the public.file_changes dataset.

examples: Updated the API route handler to validate request payloads and return consistent error responses; added unit tests for edge cases.
Refactored the auth middleware to use a shared token validation utility and improved logging for failed authentication attempts.
Replaced deprecated date parsing logic with a safer formatter and fixed timezone handling in the report generation module.
Adjusted the database migration script to add missing indexes on key lookup columns and verified schema compatibility.
Modified the React component to split large state into smaller hooks, improving readability and preventing unnecessary re-renders.
Enhanced the file upload service to stream large files, added retry logic for transient storage errors, and updated documentation.
Fixed an off-by-one bug in pagination by correcting the limit/offset calculation and added regression tests.
Updated the configuration loader to support environment variable overrides with sensible defaults and validation.
Reworked the prompt template for the AI agent to include clearer instructions and structured output formatting; added snapshot tests.
Corrected TypeScript types across the service layer to align with actual API responses and removed redundant casts.
Improved the CI pipeline by adding caching for dependencies, tightening linting rules, and ensuring consistent build artifacts.
Refined the business logic for entitlement checks to handle expired subscriptions correctly and added integration test coverage.
Optimized query performance by rewriting a complex join into two simpler queries and indexing the queried fields.
Updated the webhook controller to verify signatures, handle idempotency keys, and return standardized status codes.
Resolved a UI bug where notifications duplicated on refresh by fixing the state hydration logic and adding a component test.
Adjusted the error boundary behavior to capture async render failures and display a fallback UI without breaking navigation.
Added a new endpoint for retrieving audit logs with pagination and filtering, and documented request/response schemas.
Fixed styling regressions by updating CSS variables, removing conflicting class selectors, and validating layout across breakpoints.
Refreshed the Overlord workflow scripts to improve concurrency control, add progress reporting, and ensure deterministic ordering.
description: This column contains a brief natural-language summary of what changed in the file associated with each record (e.g., key edits made by an AI coding agent, how the file was modified, and the main outcome of those modifications) as part of the Overlord workflow for managing, coordinating, and reviewing AI-assisted code changes.

examples: Fixes a null dereference in the request handler found during AI review; adds a guard clause and updates unit tests to cover the missing payload case.
Implements the new search filter feature requested by product; updates query builder logic and extends integration tests for edge-case combinations.
Refactors the file upload pipeline to improve readability and reduce duplicate code paths; aligns with existing Overlord guidelines for maintainability.
Adjusts role-based access control checks to match the latest acceptance criteria; includes migration-safe changes and verifies via authorization test suite.
Updates the scheduler to prevent overlapping runs; resolves a timing bug reported by monitoring and adds deterministic test coverage using a fake clock.
Adds missing validation for user-provided parameters to prevent invalid state transitions; documented rationale for why the checks are necessary.
Improves test reliability by removing flaky timing assertions and switching to event-driven synchronization; confirmed by CI runs post-change.
Refines error messages returned by the API to be actionable for operators; ensures consistency with the system’s error contract and adds regression tests.
Optimizes database queries by adding an index and rewriting the join strategy; validated via query plan inspection and performance-focused tests.
Updates documentation for the public API endpoints and coding conventions; required to satisfy the Overlord acceptance checklist for traceability.
Corrects a schema mismatch between producer and consumer components; includes backward-compatible parsing and a migration note for existing rows.
Implements a feature flag to enable staged rollout of a new pricing calculation; adds audit logging and tests for both enabled/disabled paths.
Refactors the background job retry logic to use exponential backoff; prevents retry storms and improves observability with structured logs.
Addresses a failing integration test caused by environment-specific configuration; introduces safer defaults and updates test harness setup.
Enhances the AI agent review workflow by standardizing change rationale formatting; ensures future reviews can quickly understand intent and risk.
Fixes a bug in pagination where the last page could repeat results; adds unit tests for boundary conditions and off-by-one scenarios.
Adds missing changelog entries for a user-facing behavior change; required for compliance with release documentation requirements.
Improves code coverage by adding tests for newly introduced edge cases in the billing domain; confirms expected behavior across tiers.
Replaces deprecated library usage with the supported alternative; updates implementation details and verifies behavior through regression tests.
description: This column explains the rationale behind each file change recorded in public.file_changes.why—i.e., why the change was made in the context of the Overlord-managed work coordination and AI coding agent review (such as addressing a bug, implementing a feature, refactoring, improving tests/docs, or meeting acceptance requirements).

examples: High: Updates core authentication logic, changing token validation and potentially affecting all user sessions and API access.
Medium: Refactors shared utility functions; may alter edge-case behavior and requires regression testing for dependent modules.
Low: Adjusts non-functional documentation and comments; no runtime behavior change expected.
High: Modifies database migration scripts; may impact schema compatibility and downstream queries for analytics pipelines.
Medium: Changes request/response serialization in an API client; could break consumers expecting the previous payload shape.
High: Fixes a security vulnerability (e.g., input sanitization/SQL injection mitigation) that changes how external data is handled.
Low: Reformats code to match linting rules; no functional changes intended.
Medium: Updates feature-flag evaluation logic; may enable/disable behavior for certain cohorts and environments.
High: Alters business rules for pricing/quotas; affects billing calculations and downstream invoicing workflows.
Medium: Improves caching strategy with different TTLs; may impact performance and consistency under load.
High: Fixes concurrency/race condition in background jobs; prevents duplicate processing and changes job execution outcomes.
Medium: Changes event schema for message bus publishing; requires consumers to update deserialization and mapping.
Low: Removes unused imports/variables; should not affect runtime behavior.
High: Adjusts authorization middleware; can widen or restrict permissions, impacting protected endpoints.
Medium: Updates data validation constraints for user inputs; may reject requests previously accepted and affects form submissions.
High: Modifies CI/CD pipeline configuration; can change build/test coverage and deployment behavior.
Medium: Updates retry/backoff logic for external API calls; affects resilience and potential rate-limit handling.
Low: Changes UI copy/text labels; no backend behavior impact expected.
High: Refactors model/ORM mappings; can change generated SQL and alter query results across reports.
Medium: Updates logging/telemetry fields; improves observability but may affect log parsing/monitoring dashboards.
description: This column is about the impact of each tracked file change—how the modification affects the project’s behavior, code quality, functionality, and/or downstream systems when using AI coding agents.

This column contains the provider identifier for an authentication identity (i.e., which external auth/identity source—such as Google/GitHub/other OAuth provider—and the corresponding provider-specific id—are associated with that identity).

This column is about the authentication identity’s provider identifier (e.g., the external auth system such as Google, GitHub, or another IdP) used to determine where the user’s AI-agent access identity originated.

This column stores the hashed value of an OAuth client’s secret (client_secret_hash), used by the system to authenticate that a requesting OAuth client is legitimate without storing the plaintext secret.

This column is about the allowed OAuth redirect/callback URIs for an OAuth client registered in auth.oauth_clients, indicating where the authorization server is permitted to send the user (authorization code flow) or tokens back after authentication.

This column is about the OAuth grant types that are allowed for each registered OAuth client in the auth.oauth_clients table (e.g., which authentication flows/app permissions the client can use such as authorization_code, client_credentials, refresh_token, or other supported grant types).
This column contains the human-readable display name of an OAuth client (i.e., the name to identify the AI coding agent or application that is registered with Overlord’s OAuth configuration).
This column stores the OAuth client’s website or profile URL for the application registered as part of Overlord’s OAuth client configuration (client_uri).

This column stores a URI (typically a URL or data URI) pointing to the logo image for each OAuth client, i.e., the client/application registered with Overlord (auth.oauth_clients). It is used to display the client’s branding in authentication/consent or UI flows.

This column specifies the OAuth token endpoint authentication method that the Overlord-managed OAuth client uses when sending requests to the token endpoint (e.g., whether the client authenticates with a client secret using methods like client_secret_basic, client_secret_post, or via public/client credentials such as none).
This column is about the human-readable label (title/name) of an objective attachment associated with an Overlord AI coding agent workflow, used to identify the specific attachment in the system.

This column stores the file system or object-storage path (e.g., a URL or key) for each objective attachment associated with Overlord items. It points to where the attachment content lives so it can be retrieved later during management, coordination, and review workflows.

examples: Implement secure authentication for agent sessions
Generate unit tests for critical modules
Optimize database queries for faster performance
Design an incident reporting workflow for failures
Create a user-friendly UI for reviewing agent outputs
Add comprehensive logging and traceability hooks
Validate input schemas and enforce data contracts
Integrate CI/CD pipeline with automated quality gates
Implement feedback loops for agent improvement
Migrate services to a scalable container-based architecture
Harden error handling and retry logic for external APIs
Develop a cost monitoring dashboard for agent runs
Ensure compliance with security and privacy requirements
Write documentation for agent workflows and conventions
Implement role-based access control for management features
Add automated linting and static analysis checks
Create metrics for agent success rate and review outcomes
Improve retrieval-augmented generation for code context
Support multi-language code generation with shared standards
description: This column contains the title (name) of an objective associated with an AI coding agent workflow in the Overlord management and review system.
This column identifies which AI coding agent (or agent instance) is associated with each record, using an internal agent identifier used by the Overlord management and coordination layer.

This column stores the identifier of the AI coding model associated with an objective/work item (used by the Overlord management and review layer to track which model was used for an agent-driven task).

examples: Approved because the proposed implementation aligns with the coding standards in our repository and includes clear, maintainable abstractions with appropriate error handling.
Approved since the changes are limited in scope, reduce technical debt, and are backed by updated unit tests covering both success and failure paths.
Approved because the agent’s configuration recommendations match our deployment requirements (environment variables, secrets handling, and resource limits) and follow our security guidelines.
Approved: the solution provides deterministic behavior, avoids race conditions, and includes logging that will make future incidents easier to diagnose.
Approved since the agent validated assumptions against existing code patterns and ensured compatibility with the current API contracts.
Approved because the output introduces a safe migration strategy, with backward compatibility maintained and rollback steps documented.
Approved: the proposed changes improve performance by minimizing unnecessary database queries and adding targeted indexes where justification was provided.
Approved since the agent’s rationale demonstrates thorough review of edge cases (null inputs, large payloads, and malformed requests).
Approved because the agent included comprehensive documentation updates (README, inline comments, and example usage) to support maintainers.
Approved: the agent’s security assessment identified potential injection risks and mitigations were implemented using parameterized queries and input validation.
Approved because the tests are robust and cover integration points, including key workflows relevant to Overlord’s operational responsibilities.
Approved since the refactor preserves existing functionality, adheres to style guidelines, and reduces code duplication without altering external behavior.
Approved: the agent proposed sensible defaults and configuration flags, enabling safe rollout and easy tuning in different environments.
Approved because the changes include monitoring hooks (metrics/events) and clear alerts guidance, supporting our ongoing management and coordination.
Approved since the agent demonstrated consistency with prior decisions, referencing related components and ensuring interface stability.
Approved: the solution resolves the reported issue directly, includes verification steps, and avoids introducing unrelated modifications.
Approved because the agent’s approach uses proven libraries already in our stack, minimizing risk and maintaining compliance with architectural constraints.
Approved since the agent handled data integrity concerns by enforcing constraints, validating schema changes, and providing migration verification steps.
Approved: the agent’s concurrency model is sound, with proper synchronization and timeouts to prevent deadlocks and stalled jobs.
Approved because the proposed objective supports Overlord’s workflow by improving review transparency, generating traceable outputs, and maintaining audit-friendly logs.
Approved: the agent’s output includes clear rollback instructions, safe failure behavior, and confirms compatibility with supported versions of dependencies.
description: This column contains the human-readable explanation or justification for why an AI coding agent’s output, configuration, or objective was approved, providing the approver’s rationale in the context of Overlord’s management, coordination, and review workflow.
This column contains the human-readable labels (names) identifying organization-level execution targets within the “Overlord” system for managing and coordinating AI coding agent work.
This column stores the email address of a user who has been invited to join a public organization (i.e., the recipient identifier for organization invitations in the Overlord system).
This column stores the display name of an organization (e.g., a team/company) that uses AI coding agents and is represented within the Overlord management, work coordination, and review layer.

This column is about which external Git hosting/provider (e.g., GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) an organization uses for its repositories, so Overlord can integrate and coordinate AI coding agent workflows with the correct source control platform.
This column stores the URL to an organization's logo image (or icon) for use in the Overlord UI when managing and coordinating AI-coding-agent users across public organizations.
This column is about the Git commit identifier (SHA/id) for a given project checkpoint in Overlord.

examples: Updated agent review pipeline to add retry logic for flaky test runs
Refined checkpoint metadata schema to include commit SHA and target environment
Added support for parallel task execution across multiple coding agents
Improved prompt templating for clearer tool instructions during code generation
Fixed role-based permission bug that prevented reviewers from accessing logs
Introduced a new checkpoint type for dependency graph updates
Optimized artifact caching to reduce repeated downloads across runs
Enhanced failure classification to distinguish lint errors vs. runtime exceptions
Adjusted escalation policy to trigger human review after repeated agent disagreements
Added structured logging for tool calls and agent decision events
Updated workspace cleanup routine to safely remove stale temp directories
Implemented streaming updates to persist intermediate agent outputs in checkpoints
Reworked checkpoint diff display to highlight changes by file and hunk
Added validation for environment variables used by agent tool execution
Improved summarization of agent actions to fit within 280-character limit
Fixed regression where rerun-from-checkpoint ignored the original branch
Added rate limiting around external API calls to avoid throttling
Upgraded dependency versions for the orchestration runtime and test harness
Introduced a circuit breaker for repeated tool timeouts within a checkpoint
Documented checkpoint lifecycle and added UI hints for next-step actions
description: This column is about the short textual summary of each public project checkpoint, describing the change or update associated with a specific Git commit/ref within the Overlord (AI coding agents management/coordination/review) project.

This column is about the summarized statistics of code changes associated with a project checkpoint—e.g., counts of files changed and lines added/removed—for the Overlord (AI coding agent coordination and review) workflow.

This column contains the Git reference name associated with each project checkpoint in the Overlord system (e.g., a branch or tag name) that identifies which code line/version the checkpoint corresponds to. It is used to relate checkpoint records to the specific Git ref being monitored.
This column stores the HEAD commit SHA for each project checkpoint in the Overlord system (i.e., the exact Git commit hash representing the repository state captured at that checkpoint).
This column contains the filesystem paths (directory locations) for the public project resource directories used by the Overlord AI-coding-agent management, work coordination, and review system.

This column contains a human-readable label/classification for each project resource directory (e.g., the directory’s role within the Overlord AI coding-agent management, work coordination, and review workflow).
This column contains the unique identifier (key) for each project tag definition used by the Overlord management, work coordination, and review layer for AI coding-agent users.
This column contains the human-readable name of each project tag/label used to categorize and organize AI coding agent work within the Overlord management and review system.

examples: Use this tag to flag tasks that must be scheduled and executed in a specific order due to dependencies (e.g., infrastructure setup before application deploy). Include the prerequisite tag names or references when applicable.

Apply this tag for items that require human review before proceeding, such as high-impact code changes, security-sensitive logic, or modifications to shared libraries. Add reviewer notes and acceptance criteria in the description.
Mark work that is blocked by an external factor (waiting on an API response, another team, credentials, or a decision). Specify what is blocking progress and what condition will unblock the task.
Use this tag for tasks that are time-critical and should be handled within the next SLA window. In the description, include the due date/time, urgency rationale, and any escalation contact.

Tag items that are meant purely for documentation updates (README, runbooks, onboarding guides, or examples). Describe what documentation area to update and confirm whether code behavior changes are also required.

Apply this tag when the work includes refactoring for readability, maintainability, or performance improvements without changing external behavior. Mention the targeted module(s) and how behavior will be verified.
Use this tag to indicate a potential security issue or hardening requirement (e.g., input validation, authz checks, secrets handling). Include threat context and the recommended mitigation approach.

Tag for performance investigations and optimizations (latency, throughput, memory, query efficiency). Include what metrics or benchmarks should be collected and what success thresholds define completion.
Mark tasks that introduce or update automated tests (unit, integration, end-to-end). Specify the test type, affected components, and the expected coverage or assertions to include.
Use this tag for user-facing changes that affect UI/UX, API responses, or developer-facing behavior (breaking changes). Describe the impact and list migration steps or compatibility notes.
Apply this tag to indicate design/dependency clarification is needed before implementation. In the description, outline the open questions, stakeholders involved, and what decision is required.
Tag items that are experimental or exploratory (spikes), intended to validate an approach quickly. Include the hypothesis, timebox, and what outcome determines whether to proceed.
Use this tag for defect fixes of previously reported issues. Reference the ticket/bug ID, describe the observed behavior, expected behavior, and the regression check to add.
Mark tasks that require compliance or policy alignment (e.g., data retention, licensing, audit logging). Specify which policy applies and what evidence or artifacts must be recorded.
Tag work that includes database schema changes. Describe the migration steps, backward compatibility considerations, data backfill needs, and how to roll back safely (if relevant).
Apply this tag to indicate the task includes API contract changes. Include request/response changes, authentication implications, versioning approach, and example payloads if known.
Use this tag for infrastructure or deployment-related tasks (CI/CD, container builds, environment variables, helm/terraform). Specify target environments and deployment verification steps.

Tag items that require code formatting or linting enforcement (e.g., style fixes, lint rule compliance). Mention the tooling used and whether formatting changes should be isolated from functional edits.
Apply this tag when the work involves data migration or transformation with potential risk. Describe source/target formats, validation strategy, and how to verify data integrity post-migration.
Use this tag to denote completed review readiness. In the description, list what has been verified (tests passing, docs updated, security checks performed) and provide links to artifacts or PRs.
description: This column is about the textual explanation of each project tag definition (i.e., what the tag means and how it should be used) within the Overlord AI coding agent management, work coordination, and review system.

This column contains the display color associated with each project tag definition (e.g., the color used to visually represent that tag in the Overlord UI for AI coding-agent management, coordination, and review).

This column is about the local working directory path on the user’s machine (where the agent’s work is checked out or prepared) that corresponds to the Overlord project’s user context for running and coordinating AI coding agent tasks.

This column is about the project name for entries in the public.projects table, identifying individual projects such as the “Overlord” project (the management, work coordination, and review layer for people who use AI coding agents).
This column stores the Everhour project identifier used to link the Overlord project to the corresponding project in Everhour for time tracking and work coordination.

examples: On Slack • Focusing on sprint tasks
description: This column stores the default Slack status setting for the Overlord project (i.e., what status message/indicator should be used in Slack by default for people/work managed under that project).

This column stores an operations profile fingerprint for the Overlord project. It is a derived identifier that summarizes the project's operational configuration (e.g., the effective settings used to run and coordinate AI coding-agent work).

This column stores the identifier (token value) used to represent or register push notification targets for AI agent users—typically an individual device/app push credential used by the Overlord management and coordination layer.
This column is about the target platform associated with each token used by the Overlord AI coding agent management system (e.g., which environment or service the token applies to).
This column stores the timezone associated with a scheduled item (e.g., the timezone to use for interpreting the schedule times) for the Overlord AI coding-agent coordination and review workflow.
This column contains the human-readable name of a scheduled item in the public.schedule table (e.g., the title/label of a work coordination and review schedule).

examples: Customer B - AWS - Private Subnet Host
description: This column is about the human-readable label/name for a server entry in the public.servers table, used by the Overlord system to identify which remote host a configured AI-coding-agent environment connects to and manages.
This column contains the public server host/address associated with an entry, identifying where an AI coding agent’s operations (management, work coordination, or review) are executed or accessed.

This column is about the username credential used to authenticate to a server (i.e., the account name for accessing each host) for the Overlord AI agent management and coordination system, alongside related server fields such as host, host key fingerprint, and error status.

This column is about the SSH host key fingerprint used to verify the identity of the server that “host” points to, ensuring secure authentication for connections made by Overlord to AI-coding-agent users’ server environments; it stores the server’s expected fingerprint so clients can detect man-in-the-middle or host changes.

description: This column stores the most recent error message or error details related to operations or connection attempts for entries in the public.servers table (e.g., failures involving the server identified by label/host/username and its host_key_fingerprint), to help diagnose issues in the Overlord AI coding agent management workflow.
