description: This column stores the client user agent string presented by the authenticated session (e.g., browser/app details) for an Overlord-managed AI-coding-agent user login.

This column is about the session tag used to label and categorize an authentication session for Overlord (e.g., grouping sessions by user/client context or session purpose) in the auth.sessions table.

This column stores the HMAC key (secret) used to sign or verify refresh tokens for authentication sessions, ensuring that refresh tokens issued to users of the Overlord AI coding-agent management system cannot be tampered with.
This column stores the authorization scope(s) granted to an authenticated session for the Overlord AI coding agent management system (e.g., what actions/resources the session is allowed to access).

This column (public.shared_state.state_key) contains the identifier/key used to store and look up a specific shared state record for the Overlord system’s AI coding-agent management and coordination workflows.
This column is about the unique identifier of a Slack event used to deduplicate incoming Slack events in the public.slack_event_dedupe table.
This column is about identifying which Slack workspace (team) each record belongs to, by storing the team’s unique ID used to associate team metadata with that workspace.
This column is about the name of a Slack workspace associated with each team in the Overlord system, used to identify which Slack workspace the team’s bot and users belong to.

This column contains the Slack user identifier (bot user ID) associated with each record’s bot/workspace integration, identifying which Slack bot user is used by Overlord to manage and coordinate work for that team.

This column stores the Slack bot access token (a credential) associated with each record in the slack_workspaces table, used by the Overlord system to authenticate and interact with a given Slack workspace on behalf of an AI coding agent bot.

This column stores the Slack user identifier (Slack user ID) associated with a person in the database, linking that user to the Slack workspace and to the corresponding Slack account used by the Overlord management and coordination system for AI coding agent users.
This column is about the lifecycle stage (phase) of each ticket event related to Overlord’s management, work coordination, and review of people using AI coding agents.

examples: Ticket created by system: New issue reported by user for AI agent workflow.
Agent updated ticket status to In Progress and requested additional context.
Assignment changed: Ticket reassigned to agent 'Sofia' for implementation review.
Agent added comment: Proposed approach includes tests and documentation updates.
Status updated to Awaiting Review after agent completed code changes.
Agent requested clarification on requirements: asked user to confirm expected behavior.
Ticket moved to QA after agent passed initial unit tests successfully.
Agent attached logs showing failure in dependency installation and suggested fix.
Agent created a subtask for 'auth middleware' and linked it to this ticket.
Agent edited summary: Adjusted acceptance criteria based on stakeholder feedback.
Ticket status set to Blocked because external API endpoint is returning 500 errors.
Agent unblocked ticket after implementing retry logic and improving error handling.
Review completed: Senior reviewer approved changes and added minor improvement notes.
Agent updated ticket with migration steps for database schema changes.
Assignment changed: Ticket reassigned from 'Sofia' to 'Marcus' for final QA.
Agent added checklist items for regression testing and deployment readiness.
Ticket marked as Resolved after user confirmed the fix resolved the reported issue.
Agent closed the ticket and summarized outcome: root cause found and patch deployed.
Status updated to Reopened after a new failing test was discovered post-merge.
Agent notified stakeholders and added final comment with release version and rollback plan.
description: This column contains a human-readable summary describing what happened for each event in the Overlord project’s ticketing system—typically a brief description of the ticket/agent workflow action, such as creation, status updates, assignment changes, reviews, or other management and coordination activity related to users of AI coding agents.
This column contains the human-readable names of ticket statuses (e.g., the status labels that represent where a ticket is in its lifecycle) for records in the public.ticket_statuses table.

This column identifies the origin or provenance of each ticket-tag assignment record (i.e., where the assignment came from within the Overlord system or its integrations), such as the upstream subsystem/event that produced the ticket-to-tag relationship.

examples: Investigate payment webhook failures for Visa transactions
Fix UI alignment issue on ticket detail page
Add role-based access control for admin endpoints
Update onboarding email template for new customer accounts
Implement audit logging for ticket status changes
Migrate legacy tickets to new schema for Overlord v2
Review and optimize database indexes on public.tickets
Create scheduled job to sync external SLA data
Address concurrency issue causing duplicate ticket creation
Refactor notification service to reduce delivery latency
Document API rate limits and update client error handling
Backfill missing customer IDs for historical tickets
Configure SSO integration with Okta for enterprise tenants
Hotfix: correct calculation of remaining SLA time
Enable CSV export for filtered ticket views
Investigate elevated error rate in background worker queue
Update mobile deep links for support ticket actions
Implement data retention policy and cleanup task
Conduct post-incident review and create action items
description: This column contains the title of each ticket in the public.tickets table, summarizing the task or work item to be managed and coordinated in the Overlord system.

examples: Ticket is complete when the requested feature is implemented, unit tests are added/updated to cover new logic, and all existing CI checks pass on the target branch.
Deliver a working API endpoint with request/response validation, proper HTTP status codes, and integration tests that verify correct behavior for success and failure scenarios.
Code changes must include database migrations (if applicable), data backfill steps documented, and rollback instructions provided; deployment must be zero-downtime or explicitly justified.
All acceptance checks pass: linting, formatting, static analysis, and at least 80% coverage on newly added modules; failures must be addressed before marking complete.
Implement the UI changes per design spec, ensure responsive layout for desktop/tablet/mobile, and confirm form validation + error states are handled gracefully.
Feature is accepted only if end-to-end test flows succeed (auth → action → confirmation), and relevant screenshots/logs are attached to the ticket for auditability.
Fix the reported bug such that the original reproduction steps no longer fail; add a regression test reproducing the issue and verifying the expected resolved state.
Documentation must be updated: README, API docs, and any runbooks for operational procedures; links must be correct and commands must work as written.
Security criteria: no secrets committed, input sanitization applied where needed, authorization checks enforced at API boundaries, and dependency vulnerabilities are remediated or documented.
Operational readiness: include monitoring/alerting hooks (metrics, logs, dashboards) for the new behavior and document expected alerts and thresholds.
Performance criteria: queries are optimized (explain plans reviewed), avoid N+1 patterns, and benchmarks/regression results are included showing no unacceptable slowdown.
Implement feature flags for non-default behavior, default flag values preserve current functionality, and include rollback plan to disable the feature instantly.
Migration QA: validate schema changes against a staging dataset, confirm data integrity constraints, and ensure migration scripts are idempotent or safely re-runnable.
UX acceptance: accessibility requirements met (keyboard navigation, aria-labels, contrast checks), and visual differences are within approved tolerance.
Correctness criteria: handle edge cases (empty inputs, large payloads, timeouts), return deterministic outputs, and include tests for each edge case.
Observability: add structured logging for key events (request id, user id, operation id), and ensure logs do not expose PII/credentials.
Integrations: update third-party client configurations, confirm webhook signatures/verifications work, and add tests for retry/backoff behavior on transient failures.
Refactor with constraints: preserve existing external behavior, maintain backward compatibility, and include a changelog entry describing any behavioral nuances.
Release readiness: update versioning, ensure database schema matches application version, and verify a full deployment + smoke test on a fresh environment.
Completion requires code review readiness: follow project style guides, keep changes scoped, include clear PR description, and respond to review comments until approval criteria are met.
description: This column contains the criteria that define when a ticket in the Overlord (AI coding agent management/coordinator/review) system is considered complete or acceptable, typically describing the expected outcome, required behavior, and any checks or conditions needed for approval.

This column is about the Everhour task identifier (everhour_task_id) associated with a specific ticket in the public.tickets table, used to link the ticket to its corresponding task in Everhour for tracking work, status, and coordination within the Overlord AI-coding-agent management workflow.

This column is about the delegate responsible for handling or routing a specific Overlord ticket (i.e., who is assigned to coordinate, execute, or review that work item derived from the ticket record).

This column is about indicating the origin of a ticket in the Overlord system (e.g., where the work request came from such as an external issue tracker, a Slack message/channel, a specific integration, or another source of task intake).
This column is about the Slack channel identifier (channel ID) where the ticket or discussion for a given work item is posted in the Overlord AI-agents coordination workflow.

This column contains the Slack thread timestamp (thread_ts) associated with a ticket update in Slack, allowing the system to link the ticket to the correct conversation thread in the relevant Slack channel.

This column is about the type/category of AI coding agent configuration (e.g., which kind of agent behavior or workflow the user has configured) used by Overlord for managing and coordinating AI coding agents.

This column is about the human-readable label or name for a specific user agent token registered in the system (e.g., a friendly identifier to help identify what the token belongs to users or integrations that use AI coding agents).
This column stores a hashed representation of user agent tokens for the Overlord system, allowing the application to identify and validate tokens without storing the raw token values.

This column is about storing a short prefix (e.g., the beginning characters) of a user agent token hash for records in the public.user_agent_tokens table, used to help identify or look up tokens associated with users of AI coding agents.

This column is about the username (identifying the public user) whose AI agent execution targets are stored in the user_execution_targets table for the Overlord AI coding agent management and coordination system.

This column is about the external service or integration provider associated with a user’s AI coding agent setup (e.g., which platform the user connects to) for the corresponding API credentials stored alongside it.

This column is about the stored API key credential for a user’s AI coding agent integration (e.g., to authorize access to a specific external service/provider within Overlord’s management, work coordination, and review workflow).
This column stores the type/category of AI coding agent that a user wants to launch (e.g., which kind of agent “Overlord” will coordinate and manage), such as the agent’s role or product variant.
This column stores the identifier of the AI model that a user wants to run when launching an AI coding agent through the Overlord management/work-coordination/review layer.

examples: When launching the agent for a quick code search, I’ll first map the repository structure, then locate the relevant functions/constants, and finally propose a minimal patch with tests. Keep reasoning concise and avoid over-refactoring.

Goal: implement feature flag support. I should verify existing config loading paths, identify where runtime flags are read, then thread the flag through the smallest surface area. If unclear, ask for the expected behavior and edge cases.

For this agent/model, I will prioritize correctness over style. Start by reading the failing test/issue context, reproduce the bug mentally from code paths, then adjust logic and ensure the fix covers boundary conditions (null/empty, timezones, pagination).

Before writing any code, confirm types and contracts (DTOs, interfaces, API responses). Plan: trace the request from controller → service → repository, then implement changes and add/update unit tests to lock behavior.

I’m preparing launch notes to constrain scope: only modify files directly related to the requested endpoint. Avoid changing unrelated modules, and include a brief explanation of tradeoffs and any assumptions about input/output.

Approach: first determine whether the bug stems from authentication/authorization vs. business logic. I’ll inspect middleware, then validate the handler’s permission checks, and finally patch the offending logic with a regression test.

When integrating the new model adapter, I’ll compare the expected interface (methods, return types, error handling) against the existing abstraction layer. If there are multiple implementations, choose the closest one and ensure backward compatibility.

I should follow a test-driven path: identify current coverage, create or update tests that express the desired behavior, then implement the minimal changes to satisfy them. Keep the internal thinking documented as assumptions and verification steps.

For performance tuning, I’ll first profile or infer hot paths, then check for avoidable allocations, N+1 queries, and inefficient loops. Plan: propose the smallest optimization and quantify expected impact, plus add a benchmark if feasible.

This launch is for a refactor: preserve public APIs. I will introduce helper functions only after understanding usage frequency and side effects. If I can’t guarantee no behavior change, I’ll add safety tests or defer the change.

Need to draft a migration: verify current schema, identify required transformations, write forward/backward migration steps, and add data backfill logic. Handle large tables carefully (batching, locks) and document rollback strategy.

I’ll start by confirming the expected JSON schema and validation rules. Then I’ll implement parsing/validation in the service layer, add informative error responses, and ensure edge cases like missing fields and incorrect types are covered.

For UI-related code changes, first locate the component and state management approach used elsewhere. Then update rendering logic and add snapshot/unit tests. Internal notes should include any assumptions about props and data shape.

I should treat this as a security-sensitive task: inspect input handling, sanitize/encode outputs, verify authorization checks, and ensure secrets are not logged. If potential vulnerabilities are detected, propose mitigations and add tests.

Launch plan: resolve dependency conflicts. Steps: list current dependency versions, identify incompatible ranges, run through the package graph, choose the safest upgrade path, and verify build/test passes. Avoid breaking major APIs.

I will keep changes localized and consistent with existing patterns. First, inspect neighboring code for conventions (naming, error handling, logging). Then implement the requested behavior and add tests mirroring existing style.

For debugging a production issue, I’ll infer likely causal chain from logs/metrics, then narrow down possible modules by searching for those identifiers. Next, propose a targeted patch and a follow-up monitoring improvement.

I’m launching a multi-step agent: (1) analyze requirements, (2) draft a design outline, (3) implement incrementally, (4) run/adjust tests, and (5) produce a concise final summary. Internal notes should capture decisions and rationale.

When generating documentation, I’ll ensure it matches actual behavior and configuration options. Plan: verify relevant command flags/endpoints, update examples, and include troubleshooting sections for common failures and misconfigurations.

This agent needs to handle concurrency safely. I will review shared state usage, check for race conditions and locking strategy, then implement synchronization or immutability. Add tests that simulate concurrent access where possible.
description: This column is about storing the user’s “thinking” content (i.e., internal notes or reasoning text) associated with launching an AI coding agent, tied to the specific agent type and model used.
This column is about the unique audit identifier ("aud") for each record in the auth.users table, used to track/associate the user’s authentication record in the database.

This column is about the user's authorization level in the application (their assigned role within auth.users), determining what permissions/account capabilities they have in the Overlord system (e.g., which actions they can perform for managing, coordinating, or reviewing AI coding-agent work).

This column is about the email address for a user in the authentication table (auth.users), which uniquely identifies the account and is used for login and for related authentication/verification flows (e.g., tokens for email confirmation or email change).

This column is about the authentication credential for each user: it stores the user’s password in encrypted/hashed form (not plaintext), typically generated by the app’s password hashing algorithm and used to verify login attempts.

This column stores a one-time email confirmation token used by Overlord’s authentication system to verify a user’s email address during account signup. When the token is presented in an email confirmation flow, it allows the system to activate/confirm the user account; it is typically cleared or invalidated afterward.

This column is about a password/account recovery token used by the authentication system in auth.users. It stores a temporary token that a user receives to reset or recover access to their account (typically validating a recovery/reset request for the Overlord user).
This column stores a newly generated token used to verify an in-progress email address change for the auth.users table (e.g., sent to the user to confirm the updated email).

This column is about whether and how a user’s email address change is in progress (e.g., storing the pending/new email or related state for an email-change flow) for accounts in the auth.users table used by the Overlord project.
